Sarah thought about the divorce papers waiting on her kitchen table. About her daughter who hadn't called in three months. About the lump her doctor wanted to biopsy next week.

"What chapter am I in?"

"That's not for me to say. But you might want to look around. Maybe there's another book here you need."

Sarah returned her book to its shelf and wandered deeper into the library. She passed sections labeled with words in languages she didn't recognize, shelves that seemed to fade into mist at their edges. She found herself in front of a shelf marked "Alternate Histories."

She pulled a thin volume at random: *The Life of Sarah Mitchell (Version 8)*.

In this one, she'd said yes when James asked her to move to California. Different job. Different daughter—no, two daughters. Still divorced, but from someone else.

There was a Version 23 where she'd never married at all, became a painter, lived alone in Montana and apparently quite happily.

Version 104 where she'd died in a car accident at nineteen.

"There are infinite versions," Eleanor said, appearing beside her. "Every choice branches. Most people find this disturbing."

"It's—" Sarah paused. "Actually, it's kind of a relief."

"How so?"

"All the things I regret, the choices I wish I could take back—they happened differently somewhere. And the versions where things went 'better'—they still had their problems. Different problems, but problems."

She slid Version 8 back onto the shelf. "My version is just one of many. Not the best, not the worst. Just mine."

Eleanor smiled, really smiled, and for a moment she looked younger. "That's good. That's very good. Most people leave here either paralyzed or reckless. You've found the middle path."

Sarah looked around the impossible space. "Will I be able to find this place again?"

"If you need to. The library appears where and when it's supposed to."

Sarah walked back to the entrance, but paused at the door. "Eleanor? How long have you been here?"

"A very long time."

"Don't you get lonely?"

"Sometimes. But I have all the stories ever told. That's not bad company."

Sarah stepped out onto the street. Behind her, she heard the door close. When she turned around, there was only a brick wall between the hardware store and the theater.

But in her pocket, she found a library card she didn't remember taking, made of something that felt like paper but shimmered like pearl.

She walked home, past her car, to the kitchen table where the divorce papers sat. She signed them. Then she called her daughter. Then she scheduled the biopsy.

Her story continued, uncertain and singular and hers.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Weight of Small Things

I've been thinking about doorknobs lately. Not in any profound way—just noticing them. The cold brass one on my bedroom door that's been loose for three years. The smooth glass knob at my parents' house that catches light like a prism every morning. The sticky handle at the coffee shop I stopped going to, not because of the handle, but because they stopped making their scones the way I liked them.

It's strange what we touch without thinking. Doorknobs. Light switches. The same three keys on our keyboards. The handle of a coffee mug. We build entire lives through these small, repetitive contacts, wearing grooves into the world and letting the world wear grooves into us.

My grandmother kept a stone in her coat pocket for forty years. Just a smooth gray pebble she'd picked up on a beach in Maine. She'd roll it between her fingers during conversations, during mass, during the long hours sitting beside my grandfather in the hospital those last few months. After she died, my mother found it in the pocket of her good wool coat and cried harder than she had at the funeral. "I'd forgotten about this," she said, though I'm not sure she'd ever really known about it in the first place. Some objects witness our lives without anyone else noticing.

I wonder how many objects like that exist. The billions of small witnesses to private gestures, nervous habits, moments of quiet comfort that no one else sees. The pen caps chewed during difficult phone calls. The particular floorboard that creaks under your weight at 3 AM when you can't sleep. The light switch you always tap twice, a superstition you developed so young you don't remember developing it at all.

We tell ourselves that memories live in photographs, in journals, in the big obvious monuments we construct. But maybe they live more truly in the things we've worn smooth with our presence. The banister you've gripped ten thousand times. The corner of the blanket you still rub between your fingers, though you're decades past childhood. The chip in the bathroom tile you've stared at so many mornings it's become a kind of friend.

I had a friend who kept every movie ticket stub from dates with her husband—fifteen years of them in a shoebox. When I asked why, she shrugged. "I don't know. I like knowing we were there." Not remembering—she couldn't recall half the movies. Just knowing. Evidence of presence. Proof of the small decision, repeated hundreds of times, to sit in the dark together and watch stories unfold.

Maybe that's what gets me about doorknobs and light switches and all the rest. They're proof of the million tiny decisions that make up a life. Each twist of a knob is a threshold crossed, a choice made, even if it's just the choice to get out of bed or go back in. Every light switched on is a small insistence: I'm here. I'm still here. I'm making this room visible.

There's a Japanese concept—tsukumogami—objects that acquire souls after existing for a hundred years. I love this idea, though I think the timeline is off. Objects acquire souls much faster than that. They acquire them the first time someone depends on them, trusts them, incorporates them into the choreography of daily survival.

My coffee mug is eight years old, chipped on one side, the design faded almost to nothing. I have nicer mugs. I use this one every morning anyway. It fits my hand right. The weight of it, the exact curve of the handle—my fingers know it in the dark. If it broke, I'd mourn it. Not dramatically, not in any way I'd admit to most people. But I'd feel its absence the way you feel the absence of a word you can't quite remember, a gap in the texture of things.

We're all building these invisible museums, aren't we? Collections of worn-smooth objects and repeated gestures that no one else will ever fully catalog or understand. And when we're gone, most of it disappears. The cosmic joke is that we can't take it with us, but also no one else can really inherit it. You can pass down your grandmother's stone, but not what it felt like in her particular fingers, not the specific comfort it provided.

But maybe that's okay. Maybe the point isn't preservation but participation. The point is the touching itself, the using, the wearing-down and wearing-in. The point is that we were here, our hands on the world, the world's handle in our grip.

I think I'll fix that loose doorknob this weekend. Or maybe I won't. Maybe I'll keep meaning to fix it for another three years, and that will become part of what it is—the loose doorknob I always meant to fix. The one that rattles slightly when I turn it, announcing my comings and goings to an empty apartment, a small sound that means I'm home.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Thing About Doors

My grandmother collected doorknobs. Not in any organized way—she wasn't that kind of person. They lived in a shoebox under her bed, a cardboard coffin filled with brass and glass and porcelain handles that had once opened paths to rooms she'd never see again.

I found the box after she died, when I was helping my mother clean out the apartment. Mom wanted to throw it away. "Junk," she said, which is what she called most things from her childhood. But I kept it. I don't know why. I didn't even like my grandmother that much when she was alive. She smelled like mothballs and disappointment, and she had a way of looking at you that made you feel like you'd already failed at something you hadn't even attempted yet.

But the doorknobs stayed with me through three apartments and a marriage that lasted almost as long as a presidential term. My ex-wife used to joke about them. "Your weird inheritance," she'd say, shaking the box so the knobs rattled like bones. After the divorce, I found myself taking them out sometimes, late at night, holding each one and wondering about the doors they'd once belonged to.

There was a blue glass one, octagonal, that caught light like a trapped piece of sky. A brass one so heavy it could have been a weapon. A white porcelain knob hand-painted with tiny flowers that were already fading when it came into my possession.

I started researching them. Not seriously at first—just occasional Google searches, visits to architectural salvage forums, that kind of thing. But it grew. I learned that doorknobs have patents, manufacturers, periods of popularity. That the Corbin company dominated the market in the 1920s. That glass knobs were often made by the same factories that produced Depression glass. That you can date a building sometimes by its hardware.

I still didn't know which doors these had belonged to, though. My grandmother had lived in seventeen different apartments over her lifetime, moving whenever the rent increased or the landlord decided to renovate or she got into a fight with a neighbor. She'd grown up during the Depression, moved to the city right after the war, and never quite landed anywhere. Each move meant leaving another door behind, but apparently not without taking a small piece of it with her.

The strangest thing happened last month. I was at an estate sale in a neighborhood being gentrified beyond recognition—old brownstones being converted into luxury condos, that whole depressing parade of progress. In one of the buildings scheduled for demolition, they were selling off everything: light fixtures, radiators, those beautiful old doors with their solid wood and vintage hardware.

I wasn't looking for anything in particular. I just like estate sales. They're like archaeological digs through other people's lives. But then I saw it—a door with a gap where the knob should have been, and next to it, a hole of absence that matched exactly the shape of the blue glass knob in my grandmother's collection.

The odds were astronomical. The city has millions of doors. My grandmother could have lived anywhere. But I knew. The same way you sometimes know things without evidence, with a certainty that bypasses logic entirely.

I bought the door for forty dollars. The guy selling it thought I was insane. "Just the door? No frame?" But I wasn't listening. I was already thinking about how to get it back to my apartment, where I could try the blue glass knob in that empty space and see if I was right.

I was right.

The knob fit perfectly, screwing into place like it had never left. And standing there in my living room with this orphaned door leaning against the wall, suddenly complete again after decades of separation, I started crying. Not gentle tears—the ugly kind, the kind that come from somewhere below your stomach.

Because I understood then what my grandmother had been doing. She wasn't collecting doorknobs. She was collecting proof that she had existed in places, that she had touched things, that she had passed through. Every time she had to leave somewhere that might have been home, she took a small piece of evidence that she'd been there at all.

My mother called it junk because she'd spent her whole life trying to forget the instability of her childhood, the constant moving, the sense that nothing was permanent. But my grandmother had kept these pieces because she needed to remember. Not the specific apartments necessarily, but the fact of them. The fact of herself, moving through the world, opening and closing doors, entering and leaving, existing.

I still have the door. It doesn't go anywhere—it's just leaning against my wall, a functional object rendered decorative by context. Sometimes visitors ask about it, and I tell them it was my grandmother's, which is true enough. I don't mention the knobs or the improbable reunion or the fact that I've started visiting salvage yards myself, looking for other matches.

I've found two more so far. One door in Brooklyn, another in Queens. Each time the knob fits, each time that small click of connection happens, I feel like I'm doing something important. Like I'm reverse-engineering a life, building a map of places from fragments of exits.

Maybe that's all we are in the end—a collection of thresholds we've crossed, doors we've opened and closed behind us. My grandmother knew that. She knew that we're always leaving, always moving through, and that sometimes the only way to prove you were somewhere is to take a small piece of it with you.

The doorknobs are still in the shoebox. But now they're not junk.

They're proof.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Drawer That Wouldn't Close

My grandmother kept everything. Not in the precious way some people curate mementos, but in the chaotic accumulation of someone who survived a war and never quite believed in abundance again. When she died, I inherited her apartment and the impossible task of sorting through seventy years of *stuff*.

Most of it was predictable: clothes from the 1970s, mismatched dishes, plastic bags stuffed inside other plastic bags. But there was one drawer in her bedroom dresser that wouldn't close properly. I'd noticed it every time I visited, always jutting out about an inch, disrupting the otherwise neat facade of the furniture.

I yanked it open that first day of sorting, expecting another cache of expired coupons or rubber bands. Instead, I found letters. Hundreds of them, bound in bundles with rotting elastic bands that disintegrated at my touch.

The first one I opened was dated 1952, written in Polish—a language my grandmother spoke but never taught us. The handwriting was masculine, angular, urgent. I took a photo and ran it through a translation app, watching the words rearrange themselves into English on my phone screen:

*My darling Helena, The crossing was worse than I imagined. I won't describe it. You said you wanted to know everything, but some things are better left in the dark of the ship's hold...*

I sat on the floor. My grandmother's name was Helen, anglicized like so much else about her after she arrived in America. But I'd never heard about anyone crossing an ocean to meet her. The story we knew was simple: she married my grandfather in New York in 1953, had my mother in 1954, lived an unremarkable life in Queens.

The letters told a different story.

His name was Tomasz. He'd been a university student in Warsaw before the war, studying literature. She'd worked in her father's bakery. They'd met at a dance in 1945, in the rubble of reconstruction, when everyone was trying to remember how to be young. They fell in love the way people do when they've recently remembered they're not dead.

But Tomasz wanted to leave. Poland was being swallowed by Soviet influence, and he could see the shadow coming. Helena's family wouldn't go—her parents were too old, her brother too stubborn, her sister too afraid. She chose them. He went alone, promising to send for her when he got settled.

The letters arrived monthly, then weekly. He'd made it to New York. He was working in a factory. He was saving money. He'd found a room for them. Just a little longer.

Then, in 1953, the letters changed tone.

*Helena, I received your letter. I understand. I don't blame you. Your family needs you, and I have no right to ask you to abandon them for a ghost of a life with me. If this is your choice, I respect it.*

But that wasn't the last letter. They kept coming. Shorter now, more factual. He'd met someone—a Polish girl from a good family. They were talking about marriage. He hoped Helena was well. He'd always cherish their time together.

The final letter was from 1955:

*I saw the announcement in the Polish newspaper. You married a man named Henry Kowalski. I hope he makes you happy. I hope he gives you everything I couldn't. I'm putting away my pen now, Helena. This is goodbye.*

I sat there until my legs went numb. My grandfather's name was Henry Kowalski. I thought of him—a quiet man who fixed watches, who died when I was seven, who I barely remembered except for his tobacco smell and gentle hands.

I dug through more bundles. Wedged at the back of the drawer, I found letters in my grandmother's handwriting. Unsent letters. Dozens of them, written over decades.

*Dear Tomasz, I think of you every day...*

*Dear Tomasz, My daughter was born yesterday. I wonder if you have children...*

*Dear Tomasz, Henry is a good man. I wish I could love him the way I loved you...*

*Dear Tomasz, I'm old now. I wonder if you're still alive...*

The last one was dated three months before she died:

*Dear Tomasz, I never sent these letters. I don't even know if you'd want to read them. But I kept yours, and I kept my words to you, and maybe that's its own kind of faithfulness. I chose duty over love, and I don't know if that was brave or cowardly. I gave my husband loyalty, my family devotion, my daughter everything I had. But I gave you something too—a drawer in my dresser that wouldn't quite close, a space that never quite healed. I think that's where you lived, in that gap, in that inch of space that wouldn't shut. I'm tired now. Soon all the drawers will close.*

I cried then, for the first time since she died. Not for the loss of her, but for the life she carried in secret, for the love she preserved like jam in a jar, too precious to open, saved for a special occasion that never came.

I kept the letters. All of them. They're in my apartment now, in their own drawer—one I occasionally leave open an inch, on purpose, as a kind of monument to all the lives we don't live, all the versions of ourselves we keep bundled in elastic bands, hidden but not quite thrown away.

Sometimes I wonder if Tomasz kept her letters too, if somewhere there's another drawer that wouldn't quite close.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Waiting Room

The clock on the wall has been stuck at 3:47 for at least two hours. I know this because I've watched it obsessively, willing the minute hand to move, to prove that time still exists, that I haven't slipped into some pocket dimension where nothing changes and everything smells like antiseptic and old magazines.

There are six other people in this waiting room. I've named them all.

**Coffee Stain** is the man in the corner wearing a tie that looks like he used it as a napkin. He keeps checking his phone, typing furiously, then sighing and putting it away, only to pull it out again thirty seconds later. His leg bounces like a sewing machine needle. I wonder if he knows he's doing it.

**The Knitter** is an elderly woman with purple hair—the defiant kind of purple that says "I'm eighty and I'll do what I want." Her needles click in a rhythm that should be soothing but instead burrows into my skull like a metronome counting down to nothing. She's making something yellow. A scarf, maybe. Or a very long sock.

**Magazine Girl** hasn't looked up once. She's been reading the same issue of *Better Homes & Gardens* since I arrived. I saw her turn back to page one twenty minutes ago and start again. I want to ask her if she's okay, but we have an unspoken contract in waiting rooms: nobody talks unless absolutely necessary.

**The Couple** sits across from me, not touching, not speaking. They have the particular stillness of people who've run out of things to say but aren't ready to admit it. She wears a wedding ring. He doesn't. I invent stories: second marriage, lost his in the ocean, allergic to gold. None of them feel right.

**Coughing Man** does exactly what his name suggests. Every forty-five seconds, like clockwork—unlike the actual clock—a deep, wet cough that makes everyone else flinch. We've all mastered the art of not looking at him while simultaneously leaning imperceptibly away.

And then there's **The Kid**, maybe seven, drawing in a notebook with a green crayon. Just green. Every picture is green. Green dogs, green houses, green people with green smiles. His mother—Magazine Girl, I realize—doesn't notice. He's the only person in this room who seems content with the suspension of time.

I haven't named myself. Maybe I'm not really here.

The fluorescent lights hum in B-flat. I learned that from a documentary once. All fluorescent lights hum in B-flat, which means waiting rooms across the world are all singing the same note, a cosmic chorus of stagnation. If you listen carefully, you can hear it: the sound of people suspended between before and after, the liminal drone of not-quite-yet.

A door opens. We all look up—even Magazine Girl—like meerkats sensing danger. A nurse with a clipboard and a tired smile calls out: "Henderson?"

Nobody moves. 

"Henderson?" she repeats, looking around with increasing confusion.

"Wrong room," Coffee Stain finally says. His voice is startling. We don't use voices here.

The nurse frowns at her clipboard, mutters something, and disappears back through the door. It clicks shut with a finality that feels personal.

We all settle back into our positions, into our private silences. The Knitter's needles resume their clicking. Coughing Man coughs. The Kid colors a green sun in a green sky.

I think about all the waiting rooms I've sat in. Waiting to be hired. Waiting to be seen. Waiting for test results. Waiting for flights that were delayed, for dates that were late, for phone calls that never came. We spend so much of our lives waiting, treating the present as an inconvenient gap between past and future.

But maybe that's wrong. Maybe this is it. Maybe the waiting *is* the thing, not the interruption between things. Maybe we're not supposed to wish it away but to find something in it—some small observation, some fragment of beauty or strangeness that only exists in the in-between.

The Kid holds up his picture to his mother. She glances at it, nods absently, and returns to her magazine. He doesn't seem disappointed. He just starts a new picture. More green.

I fish my own phone out of my pocket and open the notes app. I start writing about the people around me, about the broken clock and the fluorescent hum and the particular quality of light that only exists in waiting rooms—neither natural nor quite artificial, a simulation of brightness without warmth.

Coffee Stain is watching me now. I can feel his eyes on my screen. Let him look. Maybe he'll end up in someone else's notes someday, in another waiting room in another city, still bouncing his leg, still checking his phone, still waiting.

The door opens again.

"Morrison?"

That's me. That's my name.

I stand up, gathering my things, and for a moment I hesitate. I want to say goodbye to these strangers, these unnamed companions in suspension. I want to tell The Kid to keep drawing green things. I want to tell Magazine Girl it's okay to stop pretending to read.

But I don't. Because that's not what we do here.

I follow the nurse through the door, leaving the waiting room behind.

The clock is still stuck at 3:47.

And six people remain, waiting, each lost in their own suspension, each convinced they're the only one who's really here.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Memory Keeper

The old woman kept memories in glass jars.

Not her own—those were fading, slipping through the cracks in her mind like sand through arthritic fingers. No, she kept other people's memories. The ones they wanted to forget.

Her shop stood on a corner that existed only on Tuesdays and Thursdays, wedged between a laundromat and a store that sold nothing but buttons. A hand-painted sign above the door read "Burden Relief Services," though most people who found it weren't looking for signs. They were looking for relief.

Today, a young man pushed through the door, shoulders hunched against rain that hadn't started falling yet.

"I need to forget," he said, because they always got right to it. No one came here for small talk.

The old woman nodded, her silver hair catching the light from lamps that burned without electricity. "Show me," she said.

He held out his hands, and between them, the memory shimmered into visibility—a technique she'd taught him without words, a knowing that happened the moment he stepped across her threshold. The memory looked like smoke and stained glass, swirling with colors she'd learned to read: deep purple for grief, red for rage, and that particular shade of gray that meant guilt.

"She didn't deserve what I said," he whispered. "My sister. The last words before the accident."

The old woman had heard variations of this story perhaps a thousand times. Last words. Wrong words. Words that crystallized into weapons when time ran out for apologies.

"The forgetting costs something," she reminded him. Always the warning. Always the same tired speech. "When I take the memory, I take its weight, but also its shape. You'll forget the bad, but also the texture of the good wrapped around it. You'll lose the exact shade of her anger, but also the precise tone of her laugh. Memories aren't surgical. They're roots. Pull one, and others come loose."

"I don't care. I can't carry this anymore."

She nodded. They never cared. Not at first.

She reached out with hands that were older than they looked, older perhaps than the building itself, and drew the memory from the space between his palms. It came reluctantly, as they always did, stretched like taffy, clinging to him with desperate fingers. But she was practiced, and it came free with a sound like tearing silk.

The young man gasped—relief, loss, or both. He'd never be able to say which.

The old woman turned to her shelves, lined floor to ceiling with jars of every size. Mason jars, medicine bottles, antique apothecary containers with Latin labels worn smooth. Each one glowed faintly from within, holding its cargo of carefully extracted pain.

She found an empty jar—sea-green glass, the size of her palm—and coaxed the memory inside. It fought, the way they always did, wanting to return home. But she whispered words in a language she didn't remember learning, and it settled, swirling, caught.

"Will you keep it forever?" the young man asked.

"Until you're ready to take it back."

"I'll never be ready."

"They all say that," she said, not unkindly. "My shelves tell a different story."

He left lighter, buoyant almost, already forgetting the weight of what he'd carried. Already forgetting her face, the shop, how he'd gotten here. By the time he reached the street, he'd remember only that he'd been running an errand, though he couldn't recall what kind.

Alone again, the old woman sat in her chair—the one that had molded itself to her shape over decades or perhaps centuries—and held the jar up to the light.

Inside, she could see everything. The sister's face, sharp with anger. The brother's words, flung like stones. The squeal of tires. The silence after. But also: birthday candles and shared secrets, inside jokes and parallel scars from learning to ride bikes together, the complicated geometry of growing up in the same small house.

She set the jar on the shelf between a bottle containing a mother's postpartum depression and a vial holding a soldier's frozen moment of cowardice.

Her shelves were very full.

Sometimes people came back. Not often, but sometimes. Years later, decades later, they'd push through her door with a confused expression, drawn by something they couldn't name. They'd look at the shelves, and their eyes would catch on a particular jar, and they'd say, "That's mine, isn't it?"

And she'd nod and take it down, and ask, "Are you ready?"

Sometimes they were. Sometimes they'd remember just enough of the shape of their loss to want it back, weight and all. She'd open the jar, and the memory would rush home like a salmon upstream, and they'd stagger under the sudden freight of feeling. But they'd stand straighter too. More complete. More themselves.

Sometimes they'd look at their jar and say, "Not yet," and leave.

And sometimes—this was rarer—they'd look at the shelves upon shelves of glowing jars and ask, "Don't you get tired? Keeping all this pain?"

The old woman would smile then, the way cliffs smile at the ocean.

"Someone has to hold it," she'd say. "Someone has to remember that forgetting is borrowed, not bought. Someone has to keep the door open for when you're strong enough to be whole again."

Outside, the rain began to fall, right on schedule. The old woman made tea in a pot older than memory and sat among her shelves of beautiful, terrible light, keeping watch over sorrows that weren't hers but were her responsibility nonetheless.

On Wednesdays and Fridays, the shop wasn't there at all.

But it was always waiting.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Library at the End of Memory

The first time Maya forgot her daughter's name, she was standing in the cereal aisle at Safeway. She'd been reaching for the granola—the kind with almonds, not pecans, because someone didn't like pecans—when the knowledge simply slipped through her mind like water through a sieve. She stood there, hand suspended, while a terrible blankness opened up inside her. 

She knew she had a daughter. Twenty-three years old. Worked in marketing. Loved old movies. Called every Sunday. But the name—

It came back thirty seconds later. Emma. God. Emma.

Maya didn't tell anyone. She was only fifty-six. It was just stress, lack of sleep, too much caffeine. Everyone forgot things.

The second time, she forgot how to get home from the grocery store. She'd driven this route a thousand times, but suddenly every street looked foreign, hostile. She pulled over, hands shaking, and used her phone's GPS. The house was four blocks away.

After that, they came faster.

***

Dr. Reeves had kind eyes and terrible news. Early-onset Alzheimer's, probably. They'd need more tests to be sure, but the cognitive assessments, the brain scans, the genetic markers—everything pointed the same direction. A slow dissolving. A gradual erasure.

"How long?" Maya asked.

"Everyone's different," he said, which was doctor-speak for *I don't want to tell you.*

Maya asked anyway. He gave her five to eight years before she'd likely need full-time care. Maybe longer. Maybe less.

She sat in her car in the parking garage for an hour, watching the concrete pillars and thinking about nothing. Then she drove home—carefully, following the GPS—and did something she'd been thinking about for years.

She started writing down her life.

***

Not a memoir, exactly. More like an inventory. An archive.

She began with the basics. Her childhood in Sacramento. Her parents' names—Richard and Susan, both gone now. Her brother Tommy, who lived in Portland and collected vintage motorcycles. Her ex-husband, briefly. Emma, extensively.

Then the smaller things. The recipe for her mother's lemon cake. The way her first apartment smelled like radiator heat and ambition. The name of the street where she'd learned to ride a bike. The feeling of swimming in Lake Tahoe in July, the cold so sharp it stopped your breath.

Every morning, she wrote. Every evening, she read what she'd written, trying to cement it, to shore up the crumbling levees of her mind.

Some days were better than others.

***

Emma wanted her to move in, to stop living alone, but Maya refused. She hired a part-time aide instead—Marcus, a nursing student with an easy laugh who didn't mind when she asked him the same questions twice. Then three times. Then more.

She kept writing. The project had evolved. She wasn't just recording memories anymore; she was trying to capture the texture of consciousness itself. The associations, the tangents, the way one thought led to another in an endless web of meaning.

She wrote about the color yellow and how it connected to her grandmother's kitchen, which connected to the smell of baking bread, which connected to her first kiss (inexplicably, but truly) with a boy named Aaron behind the school gym. She wrote about how certain songs could resurrect entire years. How the word "melancholy" tasted different from "sadness." How memory wasn't really about the past at all, but about who you were now, looking backward.

The document grew to hundreds of pages. Then thousands.

She knew, on some level, that she was building a monument to a self that was already disappearing. That she wouldn't remember writing it. That eventually, she wouldn't be able to read it.

But she kept going.

***

There was a period—months, maybe a year—when things stabilized. The disease was still there, still advancing, but slowly. She learned tricks. Routines. She labeled everything in her house. She set alarms. She told Marcus stories about her life, the same ones over and over, not remembering that she'd told him, and he never let on.

Emma visited every weekend. Sometimes Maya knew her instantly. Sometimes it took a moment. Once, terribly, Maya asked who she was, and Emma excused herself to cry in the bathroom while Marcus made tea and pretended not to notice.

Still, there were good days. A picnic in Golden Gate Park where everything felt almost normal. A movie night where Maya remembered every plot point. An afternoon going through old photographs where the memories came easily, fully, like gifts.

Maya stopped adding to the document. She read it instead, these thousands of pages of her own life, and sometimes she recognized herself. Sometimes she was reading about a stranger. Sometimes she couldn't follow the words at all, but she liked the rhythm of them, the sound they made in her mind.

***

Near the end—though she doesn't know it's near the end—Maya has a dream.

She's in a library. Infinite shelves stretching in every direction, and every book is her. Every volume contains some version of her memories, her thoughts, her self. Some are thick and detailed. Some are slim, pages blank or fading. Some are written in languages she doesn't recognize anymore.

A librarian approaches. She has Emma's eyes.

"You can't take them with you," the librarian says, not unkindly.

"I know," Maya says, and she does know, even here. "But I needed to write them down. I needed to prove they were real."

"They were real," the librarian says. "They are real. Even after."

Maya wakes up crying, though she can't remember why. Marcus brings her breakfast. The sunlight through the window is yellow—her favorite color, though she's forgotten the reason.

On the desk, a document of thousands of pages sits closed.

She doesn't open it. But sometimes, she rests her hand on it, and it feels warm.

Like a life. Like proof.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Weight of Small Decisions

I stood in the coffee shop for seven minutes trying to decide between medium and large. The barista waited with practiced patience, her marker hovering over the cup. Behind me, I could feel the weight of other people's mornings pressing against my back.

It's always been like this. Not just with coffee, but with everything. Which route to take home. Whether to text back immediately or wait. What to order at restaurants. If I should say what I'm actually thinking or the safer, smoother thing. Each choice branches into infinite futures I'll never see, alternate versions of my life splitting off like fractals, impossible to track.

My therapist says this is anxiety. My mother says I overthink. My ex-girlfriend said I was "exhausting to be around," which at least had the virtue of specificity.

I ordered the medium. I always order the medium.

Walking back to my apartment, I passed the same homeless man who's been on the corner of Fifth and Main for three years. I gave him the same apologetic smile I always give—acknowledging his existence while doing absolutely nothing about it. This is maybe the worst decision I make, every single day, and also the easiest. 

The thing about small decisions is that they feel both crushingly important and utterly meaningless at the same time. The coffee size doesn't matter. Except it determines how much caffeine I intake, which affects my anxiety levels, which influences how I interact with my coworker Dave, which could theoretically alter the trajectory of the project we're working on, which might impact whether I get promoted, which could change everything.

Or I just drink some coffee and nothing happens.

I've been reading about parallel universes. The many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics suggests that every decision splits reality into multiple branches, each one equally real. There's a universe where I ordered the large. One where I didn't go to the coffee shop at all. One where I said something to the barista beyond my order, where we struck up a conversation, where she became important to me somehow.

In infinite universes, I've made every possible choice.

This should be comforting. It isn't.

Because I'm stuck here, in this one, with this specific accumulation of decisions that have made me whoever I am. Thirty-four years old, still single, working a job that's fine, living in an apartment that's fine, drinking medium coffees and taking the same route home and never, ever doing anything unexpected.

My phone buzzes. It's my brother, asking if I want to come to his daughter's birthday party this weekend. It's a three-hour drive. I have things I should do this weekend—laundry, groceries, finally hanging those pictures that have been leaning against the wall for eight months. 

But also: my niece will only turn seven once. And my brother has been better to me than I've been to him. And I'm so fucking tired of my own company.

I type out "Yes," then delete it. Type "Can't this weekend," delete that too.

The barista had a tattoo on her wrist. I noticed it when she handed me the cup. Some kind of bird, wings spread. I wondered about the story behind it, if she chose it quickly or agonized over the design, the placement, the commitment of it. Whether she regrets it now or if it still means whatever it meant when she first got it.

I wish I could be the kind of person who gets tattoos. Who makes bold choices and lives with them, skin and all.

Instead I'm the kind of person who's been thinking about adopting a dog for two years but can't commit to the responsibility. Who keeps rewatching the same shows because new ones might disappoint. Who has a document on his computer titled "Novel Ideas" that hasn't been opened since 2019.

The coffee is already getting cold.

Here's what I know: every small decision is practice for the big ones. And I'm practicing cowardice. I'm rehearsing mediocrity. I'm getting incredibly skilled at choosing nothing, which is still a choice, just the worst kind.

There's a universe where I'm different. Where I said yes to the job in Seattle. Where I asked Rachel to stay instead of pretending I was fine with her leaving. Where I told my father I loved him before he died instead of assuming there would be more time.

But that's not this universe.

This is the one where I stand outside my apartment building, phone in hand, cursor blinking in an empty text message.

My thumbs move. "I'll be there. Wouldn't miss it."

I hit send before I can unsend it. Before I can qualify it or add conditions or give myself an exit route. The message goes, disappears into the digital void, becomes real in my brother's phone somewhere across the city.

My heart is pounding like I've done something dangerous.

Maybe I have.

I look at my coffee cup. The barista had written my name wrong—"Jon" instead of "John." An inconsequential error. A universe of difference. In some reality, I went back and corrected her. In this one, I keep walking, carrying someone else's name, feeling something shift in my chest that might be discomfort or might be possibility.

The homeless man is still on his corner. Tomorrow, I think. Tomorrow maybe I'll stop. Maybe bring him coffee. Maybe actually talk to him.

Or maybe I won't.

But I could.

And that, right now, feels like enough.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Collector of Lost Sounds

Maria kept sounds in mason jars.

Not recordings—actual sounds, pulled from the air like butterflies and preserved in glass. She stored them on shelves in her grandmother's basement, hundreds of jars labeled in her careful handwriting: *Dog barking, Tuesday morning. Teakettle whistle, sharp. Laughter, gender unknown.*

She'd discovered the ability at seventeen, reaching for her mother's voice during an argument and feeling it come loose in her hand like a piece of fruit. The words "You never listen" hung there, visible only to her—a shimmer of bronze and blue—before she trapped them in an empty jam jar. Her mother had stood frozen, mouth open, suddenly mute. The sound came back after thirty seconds, but Maria had already run to her room, holding the jar up to the lamplight, watching her mother's anger swirl inside like smoke.

Now, at thirty-four, she worked as a sound technician for films, which people found funny when she told them. She'd touch the boom mic and feel the textures of every noise—silk, sandpaper, velvet, rust. Some sounds begged to be taken. Others clung to their sources, loyal.

Her collection grew obsessively. She took the sound of rain from a funeral, the specific pitch of her nephew's first cry, the hum of the refrigerator from her first apartment. Each jar was dated, contextualized, precious.

But some sounds she couldn't capture, no matter how she tried.

Her father's voice, for instance. He'd died when she was nineteen, and she'd been so confident in her ability by then that she hadn't worried. She had a jar with him saying "Good morning, sweetheart" from three weeks before the heart attack. She'd thought that would be enough.

It wasn't.

She played it sometimes—not through speakers, but by opening the jar just a crack. The sound would leak out, fill the basement, and for six seconds she'd hear him again. Then silence. She could put the sound back, seal the jar, save it for next time. But each time she played it, the sound grew thinner, like a photocopy of a photocopy. She was losing him twice.

Marcus noticed the jars on their fourth date.

"What are these?" He'd come down to help her find a wrench, stopped at the shelves.

"A project. Art thing."

"Can I open one?"

"No!"

He'd stepped back, hands up. "Okay, okay."

She'd almost ended it there. Too dangerous, too close. But Marcus had smiled at her—not a mocking smile, a curious one—and said, "Will you tell me about it someday?"

"Maybe."

Someday came eight months later. She brought him to the basement, handed him a jar labeled *Ocean, 3 AM, no witnesses.* "Open it."

He did.

The sound of waves filled the basement—not from speakers, but from everywhere and nowhere, like they were standing on that beach. He dropped the jar. It didn't break, but the sound escaped fully, crashed around them for thirty seconds, then dissipated.

"How—"

"I don't know how. I just can do it."

Marcus sat down on the basement floor. Stood up. Sat down again. "That's... Maria, that's impossible."

"I know."

"Can you teach me?"

She'd tried. Like trying to teach someone to wiggle their ears—either you could or you couldn't. Marcus couldn't.

But he became her assistant archivist. He'd suggest sounds to collect: the ice cream truck in summer, their neighbor's terrible opera practice, the specific silence after snow. He bought her nicer jars, helped her organize by category instead of chronology.

"You should share these," he said one night. "People should hear them."

"No."

"Why not?"

Because they were hers. Because the world already took everything—time, people, moments—and she'd found a way to hold something back. Because if she shared them, they'd want more, they'd want her father's voice, they'd want to regulate her or study her or take her jars away.

"I'm not ready," she said.

Marcus nodded. He was good at accepting her borders.

The jar she never labeled sat in the back, hidden behind the others.

Inside: the sound of her own heartbeat, recorded at 2 AM on the worst night of her life, age twenty-three, when she'd decided not to keep living. She'd reached inside her own chest and pulled the sound out—a desperate experiment—and found that without her heartbeat, everything stopped. Four seconds of nothing. No pulse, no breath, no thought.

She'd panicked, opened the jar, let the sound back in. Her heart had resumed, and she'd collapsed, gasping.

The heartbeat still swirled in that jar, waiting. Insurance policy or temptation, she didn't know.

Marcus found it eventually. Of course he did.

"This one has no label."

"Don't open it."

"What is it?"

"My heart. Literally."

She explained. He went pale. "Maria, you need to destroy this."

"I can't."

"Why not?"

"Because I need to know I have a choice."

He'd held her then, and she'd heard his heartbeat against her ear—steady, solid, uncollected. A sound that existed in real-time, that she couldn't preserve, that would someday stop and leave nothing but memory.

"Promise me," he whispered. "Promise you'll choose to keep it beating."

She'd promised.

The collection continued, but something shifted. She started taking different sounds: Marcus laughing at his own jokes. Her nephew saying her name. The rainfall that didn't come from a funeral but from a picnic they'd refused to abandon, eating sandwiches under a tree.

Sounds that hurt less to preserve because they weren't replacements for living. Just evidence that she had.

Maria still keeps sounds in mason jars. But now, most days, she leaves the jars sealed.

And listens to the world directly instead.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Museum at the End

The museum appeared on Tuesday, which was unusual because there had never been a museum on Forrest Street before, and someone surely would have noticed a building appearing overnight. But there it stood: three stories of red brick with white trim, nestled between the dry cleaner and the vacant lot like it had always belonged.

Maya noticed it on her way to work. She walked this route every day, had for three years, and she *knew* what belonged here. Nothing belonged here. Just the gap between buildings where kids sometimes left their bikes.

The sign above the door read: "The Museum of Things You've Forgotten."

She should have kept walking. She was already late. But her feet stopped, and her hand reached for the brass handle, and the door opened with a sigh that sounded almost relieved.

Inside, the air smelled like old paper and birthday candles. The entryway was small, floored in black and white tiles that reminded her of something she couldn't name. A woman sat behind a desk that seemed too large for the space, reading a book with no visible title.

"Admission?" Maya asked.

"Free," the woman said without looking up. "But you can only visit once."

The first gallery was labeled "Childhood Ambitions." Maya wandered past glass cases containing tiny dioramas. In one, a small figure in a lab coat discovered a cure for sadness. In another, a child conducted a symphony made entirely of bird calls. A miniature Maya—she recognized her own gap-toothed smile—stood on a stage, performing a one-woman show to a packed theater, all the seats filled with people who finally understood.

She had wanted to be an actress. How had she forgotten that? The dream sat in her chest now like something excavated, still crusted with the soil of years.

The next room: "Words You Meant to Say."

This gallery had no cases, just slips of paper hanging from the ceiling on fishing line, thousands of them, rotating slowly in an unfelt breeze. Maya walked through the forest of regrets, reading:

*I love you too.*
*I'm not actually fine.*
*Mom, I'm scared.*
*Please don't go.*
*I see you.*

She reached out to touch one and it dissolved like cotton candy, and she remembered: her grandmother's hospital room, the machines beeping, how she'd stood in the doorway and said "See you tomorrow" instead of "Thank you for everything," because tomorrow was certain, wasn't it? Except it wasn't.

Maya's eyes burned. She moved faster through the paper forest, not reading anymore.

"Friendships You Let Die" was a hallway of photographs. Some faces she recognized immediately—Jordan from college, who she'd stopped calling after the wedding. Sam from the bookstore, whose messages she'd meant to answer. Others were blurry, and she couldn't remember their names at all, just a feeling: *We mattered to each other once.*

She almost left then. But there was music coming from somewhere above, familiar and strange at once, and she found herself climbing the stairs.

The second floor was different. Brighter. The first gallery here was labeled "Moments of Unexpected Joy," and Maya felt something loosen in her chest.

A display showed a perfect orange leaf, backlit by autumn sun. The placard read: "October 17th, walking to your car." She remembered. How she'd stopped and just looked, really looked, for thirty seconds that felt infinite.

Another case held the memory of rain on a tin roof, the summer she was twelve, reading in her cousin's attic. The sound of her best friend's laugh at a joke that wasn't even funny. The weight of a cat falling asleep in her lap. The taste of her grandfather's pancakes.

There was a whole room dedicated to "Times You Were Braver Than You Knew." Maya saw herself at seven, standing up to a bully. At nineteen, leaving home. At twenty-five, ending a relationship that was killing her slowly. At thirty, showing up to work the week after her father died because routine was a life raft and she was drowning.

"I forgot I did those things," she whispered to the empty room.

The last gallery was on the third floor. The sign on the door said: "What Comes Next."

This room was different from all the others. It was full of doors—hundreds of them, all different sizes and colors, standing free in the space without walls to hold them. Some were cracked open. Others were locked tight. Some glowed softly; others seemed to absorb light.

In the center of the room stood a single glass case.

Inside was a mirror.

Maya approached slowly. In the reflection, she saw herself, but also—something else. The actress she'd meant to become. The daughter who'd said goodbye properly. The friend who'd kept showing up. But also: the person who'd survived. Who'd made choices, some good, some terrible. Who'd felt joy in small moments and kept walking through the hard ones.

Behind her reflected shoulder, she could see the doors.

All of them led forward.

"Which one do I choose?" she asked aloud.

"You already are," said the woman from downstairs. Maya turned to find her standing in the doorway. "You choose every day. This place just helps you remember what you're choosing from."

"Will I forget this too?"

The woman smiled. "Probably some of it. That's okay. That's human. But you'll remember enough. You'll remember that you came here. That you looked back. That you still have doors ahead."

Maya nodded. She took one last look at the mirror, at all her possible selves superimposed over the actual one, and then she walked past the woman, down the stairs, through the gallery of joys and the forest of unsaid words, past the dreams she'd abandoned and the bravery she'd forgotten.

The morning sun was jarring after the museum's gentle light.

She turned to look back, but Forrest Street was just Forrest Street: dry cleaner, vacant lot, no museum in between.

In her pocket, she found a slip of paper. She didn't remember taking it.

It read: "Call someone you miss."

Maya pulled out her phone and scrolled through her contacts, past all the names that had become just names. She stopped at one.

Her thumb hovered.

Then she pressed call.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Weight of Small Things

My grandfather kept stones in his pockets. Not for any mystical reason—he wasn't the type to believe in crystals or energy or any of that. He just liked the weight of them. "Reminds me I'm still here," he'd say, patting his sagging jacket pocket that clinked when he walked.

I think about that now, sitting in my apartment with my phone face-down on the table, its phantom vibrations still pulsing through my leg even though it's three feet away. We all carry weight differently now. His stones were deliberate. My phone is supposed to make things lighter, but some nights it feels like I'm carrying the whole world's sadness in a glass rectangle.

There's a crack spreading across the screen that I keep meaning to fix. It happened three months ago when I dropped it rushing to answer a call from my mom. The call was nothing important—something about did I want her old coffee maker. The crack starts at the top right corner and spiders down toward the center, stopping just short of the camera. Through it, I read the news, check my email, watch videos of cooking recipes I'll never make, and scroll past the digitized lives of people I haven't spoken to in years but somehow can't unfriend.

My grandfather died before smartphones. Before social media. Before we all became archivists of our own lives, curating museums of breakfast and sunsets and meaningful quotes over stock photos. I wonder what he'd make of it. Probably something simple and devastating, the way old people can be when they're not trying to be wise.

The woman upstairs is walking back and forth again. I've never seen her, but I've constructed an entire life for her based on footsteps. She paces when she's on the phone—always between 7 and 8 PM, probably calling someone on the East Coast who's already had dinner. Sometimes she drops things. Once, something rolled across her floor for what seemed like minutes, a sound like distant thunder. A can, maybe. Or one of those exercise balls people buy and never use.

I do the same thing, I guess. The construction. The filling in of blanks. We're all unreliable narrators of other people's stories, making them supporting characters in our own heads. She probably doesn't think about me at all. Or maybe she does. Maybe she wonders about the person downstairs who plays the same song on repeat at 2 AM when sleep won't come. (It's "Clair de Lune," if you're wondering. The Debussy. I'm not sure why. It just feels like what 2 AM sounds like.)

There's a coffee shop I walk past every day on my way to work. Same route, same time, different lives unfolding at the same tables. There's a regular—fifties, gray beard, always by the window—who writes in a leather journal with a fountain pen. Actual ink. I've never seen him use a laptop. For two years now, I've walked past him, and we've never acknowledged each other, but he's become a fixture in my routine. Last week he wasn't there. Hasn't been there since. I think about him more than I should. I hope he's okay. I hope he's on vacation, or just found a different coffee shop, or finished whatever he was writing in that journal and doesn't need the window seat anymore.

But I worry it's something else. That's what our minds do, isn't it? Skip straight to the bad. It's supposed to be evolutionary—our ancestors survived because they assumed the rustling bush was a predator, not the wind. But now we don't have predators, just absences that our minds fill with worst-case scenarios.

I never learned his name. Never will, probably. He'll just be "the man who used to write by the window," a minor character who disappeared from my daily script.

My grandfather would know what to do about that. He knew everyone's name. The postal worker, the grocery clerk, the kid who mowed lawns in summer. He collected names the way some people collect stamps. Said it cost nothing to know what to call someone, but it cost them something when you didn't bother to ask.

I don't know my neighbors' names. Not the woman upstairs with her pacing and her phantom rolling cans. Not the couple next door who fight in a language I don't recognize but in a tone I understand perfectly. Not the person across the hall who cooks something with curry every Sunday that makes the whole floor smell like warmth and complexity.

We live stacked on top of each other, separated by drywall and politeness, closer than humans have ever been and somehow more isolated. My grandfather lived in the same house for fifty years. The same neighbors. The same route to the same store. The same stones in his pockets.

I've moved six times in eight years.

The crack in my phone screen catches the light sometimes and throws tiny rainbows across my wall. That's something, I guess. Beauty in broken things. Or maybe I'm just romanticizing neglect, making poetry out of procrastination.

Tomorrow I'll walk past the coffee shop again. Maybe the man will be there, and I'll feel that small relief that comes from continuity. Or maybe he won't, and I'll carry that little absence with me, another small weight in my pocket.

Not stones. Not exactly. But something just as real.

Something that reminds me I'm still here, paying attention, noticing the gaps where things used to be.

My phone vibrates. Actually vibrates this time, not phantom.

I let it ring.

samples.result:
# The Museum at the Edge of Sleep

I keep having this dream where I'm walking through a museum that doesn't exist. At least, I don't think it exists. I've never been there while awake, but in sleep, I know every corridor, every room, every painting on the walls.

The entrance is always the same: a revolving door that moves too slowly, glass panels thick as aquarium walls. Outside is nothing—not darkness, not light, just a gray absence that doesn't bear thinking about. Inside, the marble floors gleam like ice under yellow lamplight that seems to come from nowhere in particular.

The first gallery displays portraits of people I almost recognize. Not quite my grandmother, but someone with her disappointed mouth. Not my third-grade teacher, but those same wire-rimmed glasses, that same posture of exhausted patience. The paintings' eyes don't follow me—that would be too obvious, too expected. Instead, they look just past me, at something over my left shoulder, and I can never turn around fast enough to see what they're seeing.

In the second gallery, there are landscapes of places I've never been. Or have I? A cornfield under bruised purple clouds—did I see that once from a highway in Nebraska? A narrow street slick with rain, laundry hanging between balconies—is that from a movie I watched half-asleep, or somewhere I walked through in another dream? Memory and imagination tangle together here like headphone cords in a pocket.

There's a sculpture garden in the center of the museum, under a glass ceiling that shows a sky perpetually at dusk. The sculptures are all of ordinary objects made monumental: a bronze coffee cup the size of a car, a marble telephone receiver you could walk through like a tunnel, a stone remote control with buttons worn smooth by invisible hands. I always want to touch them, but I never do. Some prohibition I can't name keeps my hands at my sides.

The third gallery is the one that changes. Sometimes it's full of mirrors, but not reflective ones—they show me as I was, or as I might have been. Me at seven with scraped knees. Me at forty with gray hair I don't have yet. Me in a wedding dress I never wore, marrying someone whose face blurs when I try to focus on it. Me old, me young, me different, me same.

Other times, the third gallery contains objects I've lost. Not just lost—forgotten. A keychain from college I haven't thought about in years. A jacket I loved as a teenager. Letters I never sent. Words I meant to say but swallowed instead. They're displayed in glass cases like archaeological artifacts, with little placards I can never quite read. The text swims and shifts, the way words do in dreams.

There's always music playing somewhere, faint enough that I can't identify the song. It might be something from my childhood, or it might be something that hasn't been written yet. The melody makes me feel something I don't have a name for—not quite nostalgia, not quite longing. Homesickness for a place I've never lived.

I've learned the museum's rules through repetition. Don't run. Don't shout. Don't try to leave the way you came in. Don't ask the other visitors questions—and yes, there are other visitors, shadow-people who drift through rooms without making sound, their faces turned away, their purposes unknown.

The fourth gallery is always empty. Just white walls and that yellow light and the sound of my footsteps echoing. But I feel watched here more than anywhere else. The emptiness is too complete, too deliberate. Something should be here. Something is missing. The absence has weight and shape.

Sometimes I find a door I haven't noticed before. It's always different—sometimes red, sometimes wood, sometimes metal. It's always slightly ajar. Yellow light leaks through the crack, or maybe darkness, or maybe something else. I've never opened it. I've reached for the handle a thousand times, and every time I wake up just before my fingers touch it.

What would be on the other side? Another gallery? The exit? Something that would make sense of all the other rooms? Or would it just lead deeper into the architecture of my sleeping mind, more corridors, more rooms, more almost-familiar things?

Last night in the dream, I stood in front of that door for what felt like hours. My hand was six inches from the handle. I could hear something on the other side—voices, maybe, or music, or just the sound of breathing. The yellow light pulsed like a heartbeat.

"What are you waiting for?" someone asked behind me.

I turned, but no one was there. Just the empty gallery, the white walls, the too-bright light.

When I turned back, the door was closed completely. No light leaked through. The handle had vanished. Just smooth wood or metal or whatever it was made of.

I woke up with my arm stretched out in front of me, reaching for something in the dark of my bedroom. My heart was racing. I felt like I'd missed something important, some appointment I'd been supposed to keep.

Tonight I'll go back. I always do. The revolving door will turn slowly, and I'll step into the marble lobby, and I'll walk through the portraits and the landscapes and the sculpture garden. I'll see myself in the mirrors or search for my lost things in their glass cases. I'll stand in the empty fourth gallery and feel its weighted absence.

And maybe tonight I'll open the door.

Or maybe I'll wake up reaching, again, forever almost touching something I'll never quite grasp.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Museum of Forgotten Gestures

The museum opened on a Tuesday in March, though nobody could agree on which year. Mrs. Chen, who'd lived on the corner since 1967, swore it had always been there, nestled between the laundromat and the bodega. But her grandson insisted he'd walked past an empty lot just last week, thinking about how someone should really build something there.

The sign above the door read "Museum of Forgotten Gestures" in letters that seemed to shift between bronze and copper depending on the light. Or your mood. The curator, a person of indeterminate age who introduced themselves simply as "V," stood behind a desk made of what looked like compressed memories.

"Admission is free," V said to the first visitor, a tired accountant named Marcus who'd ducked in to escape the rain. "But you must leave something behind."

"Like my umbrella?"

"Like the way you used to wave goodbye to your daughter when she was small. You don't wave like that anymore. She's sixteen now. You kind of just nod."

Marcus froze, water dripping from his sleeve onto the marble floor, where it pooled into the shape of a tiny hand.

The museum wasn't large, but it contained multitudes. The first gallery displayed "Telephone Gestures, 1876-2010." Behind glass cases sat curled fingers, forever twisted into the shape of rotary dials. Hands frozen mid-cradle, cradling receivers between shoulder and ear. The placard explained that 3.7 billion people once held phones this way, and now almost none did.

In the corner, a holographic teenager from 2008 demonstrated the thumb-and-pinky "call me" gesture, already as ancient as hieroglyphics to her 2024 counterpart.

Marcus drifted to the next room: "Courtship Rituals."

Here, mannequins demonstrated the art of the calling card. The precise angle at which a Victorian woman would tilt her fan—each position a complete sentence. The elaborate choreography of a 1950s couple at a malt shop, sharing a milkshake with two straws, a performance so specific it required its own notation system.

"We lost seventeen distinct handshake variations in the pandemic alone," V appeared beside him, silent as thought. "The dap, the soul shake, the complex sequences friends spent years perfecting. Many people simply... forgot the steps."

"Do they want them back?"

"Some do. Some don't. The museum doesn't judge. We merely preserve."

The third gallery was called "Occupational Extinctions." 

A lamplighter's reach, stretching upward with his pole. A switchboard operator's hundred-fingered dance. The particular way a typist's pinky would slam the carriage return—that satisfying thrust leftward, now rendered obsolete by a computer key barely pressed. Video screens showed factory workers whose hands moved in synchronization with machines that no longer existed, their choreography purposeless now, yet still somehow beautiful.

Marcus found himself in front of a display labeled "The Milk Chute Crouch, 1930-1970." A black-and-white photograph showed a housewife bending in a specific way to retrieve bottles from a small door. The posture was so particular, so purpose-built for an extinct domestic architecture, that no living person would ever bend quite that way again.

"Why does this make me sad?" Marcus whispered.

V smiled. "Because you're understanding. Every gesture is a little language. When it dies, we lose a way of being human."

They walked through "Children's Games": the thumb-wrestling stance, the cat's cradle configurations, the precise finger positions for jacks, marbles, hopscotch. Each year, fewer children inherited these specific biomechanics. Soon, no human body would remember them.

The "Religious Gestures" wing was vast and hushed. Mudras from disappeared Buddhist lineages. The exact Christian genuflections of forgotten orders. The hand-washing ritual of a priesthood that ended in 1347. Sign of the cross variations, each sect's version slightly different—fifteen ways to touch forehead, chest, shoulders, each one once considered the only true way.

Marcus paused before a small exhibit: "Tucking In, Various Methods, 1600-Present."

It showed parents through centuries, demonstrating the specific way they'd tucked blankets around their children. The hospital corners of the 1940s. The burrito-wrap of the 2010s. Each generation's hands moved differently, pulled the covers with different tensions, patted and smoothed according to unwritten rules they'd learned from their parents, who'd learned from theirs.

"I still tuck Emma in sometimes," Marcus said. "She pretends to hate it."

"How do you do it?"

Marcus's hands moved involuntarily, miming the gesture. Pull, smooth, pat-pat, kiss the forehead.

"Interesting. You smooth three times. Your mother smoothed twice. Your grandmother five times. The gesture evolves."

"How could you possibly know—"

But V had already moved to the final gallery.

"Departures and Returns" read the sign above the archway.

This room held farewells. The elaborate hat-doffing of gentlemen. The curtsy. The bow—a thousand variations catalogued by culture and century and class. The military salute's slow standardization. The way people used to stand on train platforms, waving white handkerchiefs until the train became a dot, even though the passenger couldn't possibly still see them.

"We don't wave like that anymore," V said. "We know the person is checking their phone."

The final display case was empty, waiting.

"For what?" Marcus asked.

"For whatever we're doing now that we don't realize we're doing. In fifty years, someone will say, 'Remember when people used to swipe? Actually moved their thumbs like that, thousands of times a day?' And their children will laugh because they'll just think commands at their devices, and the thumb-swipe will join all of these."

Marcus stood there, dripping, thinking about Emma. How he did just nod now when she left for school. When had he stopped the big wave? What year had he decided he was too old, or she was too old, or the gesture was too old?

"Can I..." he started.

"The exit is through there," V pointed. "You'll find yourself exactly where you need to be."

Marcus walked out a door that hadn't been there before and found himself not on the street, but in his own driveway. Emma was just leaving, backpack slung over one shoulder.

He raised his hand high, fingers spread wide, and waved the way he used to—big and embarrassing and full of love.

She stopped, confused, then broke into a grin.

And waved back the same way.

Behind them, in a museum that may or may not exist, V added a new notecard to a waiting exhibit: "The Revival, Witnessed, Today."

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Curator of Lost Hours

The clock repair shop on Meridian Street doesn't open at any particular time. Mrs. Chen has been walking past it for seventeen years, and she's never seen a pattern. Sometimes the lights glow amber at dawn. Sometimes the door remains locked until twilight. Once, she's certain, it stayed shuttered for an entire week, though her husband insists it was only three days.

Today, it's open at 2:47 PM.

She knows this because she's been checking her watch obsessively since the diagnosis. Stage three. Six months, maybe eight. Time, suddenly, has become the only thing that matters, and paradoxically, the only thing she cannot control.

The bell above the door releases a sound that isn't quite a chime. More like a sigh. Inside, the shop breathes with the accumulated tick-tick-ticking of hundreds of clocks, none of them synchronized, creating a rainfall of seconds that fall at different speeds.

Behind the counter sits a young man who looks exactly like the old man who was there when she first passed this shop in 2007. Same wire-rimmed glasses. Same ink stains on his fingers.

"I need more time," she says, then laughs bitterly at herself. "Sorry. I don't know why I came in. I don't even have a broken watch."

The man smiles gently. "Most people who come here don't."

He gestures to the forest of timepieces surrounding them. Grandfather clocks with faces painted like moons. Pocket watches dangling from invisible threads. Wristwatches arranged in spiral patterns on velvet boards. Digital displays showing times from time zones that might not exist.

"I collect lost hours," he says. "The ones that slip between moments. The hours people waste, the minutes they wish they could take back, the seconds they didn't notice passing."

"That's not how time works."

"Isn't it?" He stands, moving to a shelf crowded with small glass vials. Each one seems to contain something between liquid and light. "This one is from a Tuesday in March, 2003. Someone spent it arguing about something they can't even remember now. This one—" he touches another "—is a Sunday morning someone meant to call their mother but didn't. She died that Thursday."

Mrs. Chen feels her throat tighten. "You can't sell lost time back to people."

"No," he agrees. "But I can show you where yours went."

He pulls out a leather-bound ledger, flips through pages dense with handwriting in various inks, and stops. His finger traces down a column.

"You've lost 43 hours to traffic in the last year. 127 hours to scrolling through your phone looking at nothing in particular. 89 hours to worrying about things that never happened."

"Stop."

"You've lost 312 hours to sleep you didn't need, used as escape. 67 hours to conversations with people you don't like about subjects you don't care about. 891 hours—"

"I said stop."

But her voice is weak, because she wants to know. Needs to know.

"891 hours to thinking about painting again, but never picking up a brush."

The shop seems to contract around her. All those clocks, all those stolen seconds, pressing in.

"I was going to," she whispers. "After I retired. After my husband's business stabilized. After the kitchen renovation. After—"

"After," the man says softly, "is where hours go to die."

He closes the ledger, and when he looks at her, his eyes are impossibly old. Ancient. Sad.

"I can't give you more time, Mrs. Chen. That's not what I do. But I can show you what you do with what you have left."

He picks up a clock from the counter—a simple thing, wooden, with a white face and black hands. He turns it around. The back is a mirror.

"Every moment you're given has two faces," he says. "The one that passes, and the one you choose to meet it with. You can spend your six months, your eight months, your whatever-you-have thinking about the time you wasted. Or—"

He leaves the thought unfinished.

Mrs. Chen looks at her reflection in the back of the clock. She looks tired. Gray. Faded, like a photograph left too long in sunlight.

Then she notices something. In the reflection, just over her shoulder, she can see one of the shop's windows. Through it, the street outside glows with late afternoon sun, golden and thick as honey. How had she not noticed that light when she walked in?

"The art supply store," she says suddenly. "On Fifth. Is it still there?"

The man nods.

"What time do they close?"

He glances at the chaos of clocks around them, all telling different truths. "For you? They're open as long as you need them to be."

Mrs. Chen realizes she's crying, but it feels different than it has for the past three weeks since the diagnosis. Lighter somehow. Like something releasing rather than breaking.

"How much do I owe you?"

"You already paid," he says. "Everyone does. The question is what you'll buy with what's left."

The bell-sigh sounds again as she opens the door. The golden light hits her face, and she remembers—really remembers—what it felt like to be young and standing before a blank canvas, terrified and exhilarated, with nothing but possibility ahead.

Behind her, in the shop, the young-old man makes a note in his ledger, then carefully places a new vial on the shelf. Inside it, something luminous swirls.

It's labeled: *One afternoon in autumn. Found, not lost. Price: Paid in full.*
